explanation: Peter constructs an elaborate theory about Clarissa's internal philosophy, imagining her inner monologue ('As we are a doomed race...'). The thought stream then associates to Sylvia's death, then to Clarissa's enjoyment of life, and finally to her daughter Elizabeth.
evidence: Constructive fantasy of another character's thoughts ('possibly she said to herself...'), Associative drift from philosophy to memory (Sylvia's death) to present observation (Elizabeth), Resigned, internal conclusion ('Ah well, so be it.')

passage: The compensation of growing old, Peter Walsh thought, coming out of Regent’s Park, and holding his hat in hand, was simply this; that the passions remain as strong as ever, but one has gained--at last!--the power which adds the supreme flavour to existence,--the power of taking hold of experience, of turning it round, slowly, in the light. A terrible confession it was (he put his hat on again), but now, at the age of fifty-three one scarcely needed people any more. Life itself, every moment of it, every drop of it, here, this instant, now, in the sun, in Regent’s Park, was enough. Too much indeed. A whole lifetime was too short to bring out, now that one had acquired the power, the full flavour; to extract every ounce of pleasure, every shade of meaning; which both were so much more solid than they used to be, so much less personal.
explanation: The narrator frames the thought ('Peter Walsh thought'), but the content quickly shifts into Peter's subjective, philosophical reflection on aging, marked by his own exclamations and rhythmic emphasis.
evidence: Narrator tag ('Peter Walsh thought') blending into character idiom, Exclamatory interruptions ('--at last!--'), Repetitive, intensifying syntax ('every moment of it, every drop of it, here, this instant, now')

passage: It was impossible that he should ever suffer again as Clarissa had made him suffer. For hours at a time (pray God that one might say these things without being overheard!), for hours and days he never thought of Daisy. Could it be that he was in love with her then, remembering the misery, the torture, the extraordinary passion of those days? It was a different thing altogether--a much pleasanter thing--the truth being, of course, that now _she_ was in love with _him_. And that perhaps was the reason why, when the ship actually sailed, he felt an extraordinary relief, wanted nothing so much as to be alone; was annoyed to find all her little attentions--cigars, notes, a rug for the voyage--in his cabin. Every one if they were honest would say the same; one doesn’t want people after fifty; one doesn’t want to go on telling women they are pretty; that’s what most men of fifty would say, Peter Walsh thought, if they were honest. But then these astonishing accesses of emotion--bursting into tears this morning, what was all that about? What could Clarissa have thought of him? thought him a fool presumably, not for the first time. It was jealousy that was at the bottom of it--jealousy which survives every other passion of mankind, Peter Walsh thought, holding his pocket-knife at arm’s length. She had been meeting Major Orde, Daisy said in her last letter; said it on purpose he knew; said it to make him jealous; he could see her wrinkling her forehead as she wrote, wondering what she could say to hurt him; and yet it made no difference; he was furious! All this pother of coming to England and seeing lawyers wasn’t to marry her, but to prevent her from marrying anybody else. That was what tortured him, that was what came over him when he saw Clarissa so calm, so cold, so intent on her dress or whatever it was; realising what she might have spared him, what she had reduced him to--a whimpering, snivelling old ass. But women, he thought, shutting his pocket-knife, don’t know what passion is. They don’t know the meaning of it to men. Clarissa was as cold as an icicle. There she would sit on the sofa by his side, let him take her hand, give him one kiss--Here he was at the crossing.
explanation: Peter's thoughts spiral from rationalizing his feelings for Daisy to a sudden, intense flare of jealousy and anger. He constructs a fantasy of Daisy writing a letter to hurt him, and the syntax becomes increasingly fragmented and exclamatory as his anger rises.
evidence: Self-interrogation ('what was all that about?'), Constructive fantasy of an unseen event ('he could see her wrinkling her forehead as she wrote'), Self-deprecating internal insults ('a whimpering, snivelling old ass'), Abrupt interruption of thought by external reality ('Here he was at the crossing.')

passage:
A sound interrupted him; a frail quivering sound, a voice bubbling up without direction, vigour, beginning or end, running weakly and shrilly and with an absence of all human meaning into

    ee um fah um so     foo swee too eem oo--

the voice of no age or sex, the voice of an ancient spring spouting from the earth; which issued, just opposite Regent’s Park Tube station from a tall quivering shape, like a funnel, like a rusty pump, like a wind-beaten tree for ever barren of leaves which lets the wind run up and down its branches singing

    ee um fah um so     foo swee too eem oo

and rocks and creaks and moans in the eternal breeze. Through all ages--when the pavement was grass, when it was swamp, through the age of tusk and mammoth, through the age of silent sunrise, the battered woman--for she wore a skirt--with her right hand exposed, her left clutching at her side, stood singing of love--love which has lasted a million years, she sang, love which prevails, and millions of years ago, her lover, who had been dead these centuries, had walked, she crooned, with her in May; but in the course of ages, long as summer days, and flaming, she remembered, with nothing but red asters, he had gone; death’s enormous sickle had swept those tremendous hills, and when at last she laid her hoary and immensely aged head on the earth, now become a mere cinder of ice, she implored the Gods to lay by her side a bunch of purple heather, there on her high burial place which the last rays of the last sun caressed; for then the pageant of the universe would be over. As the ancient song bubbled up opposite Regent’s Park Tube station still the earth seemed green and flowery; still, though it issued from so rude a mouth, a mere hole in the earth, muddy too, matted with root fibres and tangled grasses, still the old bubbling burbling song, soaking through the knotted roots of infinite ages, and skeletons and treasure, streamed away in rivulets over the pavement and all along the Marylebone Road, and down towards Euston, fertilising, leaving a damp stain. Still remembering how once in some primeval May she had walked with her lover, this rusty pump, this battered old woman with one hand exposed for coppers the other clutching her side, would still be there in ten million years, remembering how once she had walked in May, where the sea flows now, with whom it did not matter--he was a man, oh yes, a man who had loved her. But the passage of ages had blurred the clarity of that ancient May day; the bright petalled flowers were hoar and silver frosted; and she no longer saw, when she implored him (as she did now quite clearly) “look in my eyes with thy sweet eyes intently,” she no longer saw brown eyes, black whiskers or sunburnt face but only a looming shape, a shadow shape, to which, with the bird-like freshness of the very aged she still twittered “give me your hand and let me press it gently” (Peter Walsh couldn’t help giving the poor creature a coin as he stepped into his taxi), “and if some one should see, what matter they?” she demanded; and her fist clutched at her side, and she smiled, pocketing her shilling, and all peering inquisitive eyes seemed blotted out, and the passing generations--the pavement was crowded with bustling middle-class people--vanished, like leaves, to be trodden under, to be soaked and steeped and made mould of by that eternal spring--

    ee um fah um so     foo swee too eem oo
explanation: This is a famous instance of literary impressionism and space-montage. The narrator takes over to render the sensory and cosmic impression of the singing woman, using dense, subjective imagery. This passage serves as the connective tissue (space-montage) that bridges Peter Walsh's consciousness to Rezia Warren Smith's.
evidence: Dense, synesthetic, and cosmic imagery ('age of tusk and mammoth', 'mere cinder of ice'), Typographic spacing to represent the non-verbal sound of the song, Functions as a spatial transition between two characters' interiorities
notes: While the narrator is dominant, Humphrey includes this under SOC techniques because the imagery renders a highly subjective, impressionistic perception of reality that blurs the boundaries between perceiver and perceived.

passage: Oh poor old wretch! Suppose it was a wet night? Suppose one’s father, or somebody who had known one in better days had happened to pass, and saw one standing there in the gutter? And where did she sleep at night?
explanation: Following her spoken dialogue, Rezia's unspoken thoughts are presented directly through a series of empathetic, anxious questions.
evidence: Unmediated internal questions, Exclamatory empathy ('Oh poor old wretch!')

passage: Since she was so unhappy, for weeks and weeks now, Rezia had given meanings to things that happened, almost felt sometimes that she must stop people in the street, if they looked good, kind people, just to say to them “I am unhappy”; and this old woman singing in the street “if some one should see, what matter they?” made her suddenly quite sure that everything was going to be right. They were going to Sir William Bradshaw; she thought his name sounded nice; he would cure Septimus at once. And then there was a brewer’s cart, and the grey horses had upright bristles of straw in their tails; there were newspaper placards. It was a silly, silly dream, being unhappy.
explanation: The narrator describes Rezia's psychological state, but the text slides into her direct perceptions and hopeful rationalizations. The sight of the brewer's cart and placards freely associates with her sudden, fragile sense of optimism.
evidence: Integration of external sensory details (brewer's cart, placards) into the emotional flow, Childlike rationalization ('she thought his name sounded nice; he would cure Septimus at once'), Internal conclusion ('It was a silly, silly dream, being unhappy.')

passage: So they crossed, Mr. and Mrs. Septimus Warren Smith, and was there, after all, anything to draw attention to them, anything to make a passer-by suspect here is a young man who carries in him the greatest message in the world, and is, moreover, the happiest man in the world, and the most miserable? Perhaps they walked more slowly than other people, and there was something hesitating, trailing, in the man’s walk, but what more natural for a clerk, who has not been in the West End on a weekday at this hour for years, than to keep looking at the sky, looking at this, that and the other, as if Portland Place were a room he had come into when the family are away, the chandeliers being hung in holland bags, and the caretaker, as she lets in long shafts of dusty light upon deserted, queer-looking arm-chairs, lifting one corner of the long blinds, explains to the visitors what a wonderful place it is; how wonderful, but at the same time, he thinks, as he looks at chairs and tables, how strange.
explanation: The narrator observes the couple from the outside, adopting the hypothetical perspective of a 'passer-by', but then seamlessly slides into Septimus's own subjective, alienated perception of Portland Place ('he thinks... how strange').
evidence: Narratorial framing ('was there, after all, anything to draw attention to them'), Extended simile describing Septimus's internal state ('as if Portland Place were a room...'), Shift into present-tense character thought at the end ('he thinks... how strange')
notes: A hybrid passage that begins as omniscient description/hypothetical observation and ends by dipping into Septimus's consciousness.

passage: To look at, he might have been a clerk, but of the better sort; for he wore brown boots; his hands were educated; so, too, his profile--his angular, big-nosed, intelligent, sensitive profile; but not his lips altogether, for they were loose; and his eyes (as eyes tend to be), eyes merely; hazel, large; so that he was, on the whole, a border case, neither one thing nor the other, might end with a house at Purley and a motor car, or continue renting apartments in back streets all his life; one of those half-educated, self-educated men whose education is all learnt from books borrowed from public libraries, read in the evening after the day’s work, on the advice of well-known authors consulted by letter. As for the other experiences, the solitary ones, which people go through alone, in their bedrooms, in their offices, walking the fields and the streets of London, he had them; had left home, a mere boy, because of his mother; she lied; because he came down to tea for the fiftieth time with his hands unwashed; because he could see no future for a poet in Stroud; and so, making a confidant of his little sister, had gone to London leaving an absurd note behind him, such as great men have written, and the world has read later when the story of their struggles has become famous. London has swallowed up many millions of young men called Smith; thought nothing of fantastic Christian names like Septimus with which their parents have thought to distinguish them. Lodging off the Euston Road, there were experiences, again experiences, such as change a face in two years from a pink innocent oval to a face lean, contracted, hostile.
explanation: The narrator provides a biographical and psychological summary of Septimus's past. While highly mediated by the narrator, it describes the formation of his inner life and consciousness.
evidence: Strong narratorial voice summarizing years of experience, Psycho-narration describing his motivations ('because he could see no future for a poet'), External description of physical changes reflecting internal trauma ('change a face... to a face lean, contracted, hostile')
notes: This borders on conventional exposition, but under Humphrey's taxonomy, omniscient description of the formation of a character's psychological state is included as a foundational SOC technique.

passage: The War had taught him. It was sublime. He had gone through the whole show, friendship, European War, death, had won promotion, was still under thirty and was bound to survive. He was right there. The last shells missed him. He watched them explode with indifference.
explanation: The narrator adopts Septimus's detached, rationalizing perspective on his own survival. The short, declarative sentences mimic his emotionally numb state of mind, presenting his internal logic without explicit reporting tags.
evidence: Third-person pronouns ('he') but character's diction ('gone through the whole show'), Short, clipped syntax reflecting his emotional detachment, Absence of narratorial judgment or framing

passage: For now that it was all over, truce signed, and the dead buried, he had, especially in the evening, these sudden thunder-claps of fear. He could not feel. As he opened the door of the room where the Italian girls sat making hats, he could see them; could hear them; they were rubbing wires among coloured beads in saucers; they were turning buckram shapes this way and that; the table was all strewn with feathers, spangles, silks, ribbons; scissors were rapping on the table; but something failed him; he could not feel. Still, scissors rapping, girls laughing, hats being made protected him; he was assured of safety; he had a refuge. But he could not sit there all night. There were moments of waking in the early morning. The bed was falling; he was falling. Oh for the scissors and the lamplight and the buckram shapes!
explanation: The passage formally enacts Septimus's rising panic. The syntax becomes repetitive ('He could not feel'), and the sensory details of the hat-making are listed as anchors he uses to stave off his anxiety. It culminates in an exclamation that borders on direct interior monologue.
evidence: Repetition of the phrase 'he could not feel' mimicking an intrusive thought, Sensory cataloging ('feathers, spangles, silks, ribbons') used as a psychological defense mechanism, Exclamatory shift at the end ('Oh for the scissors...') indicating a breakdown of narratorial distance
notes: A strong example of Steinberg's simulation of a state of mind, where the rhythm and focus of the prose enact the character's desperate attempt to ground himself.

passage: But beauty was behind a pane of glass. Even taste (Rezia liked ices, chocolates, sweet things) had no relish to him. He put down his cup on the little marble table. He looked at people outside; happy they seemed, collecting in the middle of the street, shouting, laughing, squabbling over nothing. But he could not taste, he could not feel. In the tea-shop among the tables and the chattering waiters the appalling fear came over him--he could not feel. He could reason; he could read, Dante for example, quite easily (“Septimus, do put down your book,” said Rezia, gently shutting the _Inferno_), he could add up his bill; his brain was perfect; it must be the fault of the world then--that he could not feel.
explanation: The narrative voice merges completely with Septimus's internal logic and terror regarding his emotional numbness. The rationalization ('his brain was perfect; it must be the fault of the world then') is purely his own unspoken deduction.
evidence: Subjective perception of the outside world ('happy they seemed... squabbling over nothing'), Internal logical deduction presented without narratorial mediation ('it must be the fault of the world then'), Repetition of the core anxiety ('he could not feel')

passage: She respected these Englishmen, and wanted to see London, and the English horses, and the tailor-made suits, and could remember hearing how wonderful the shops were, from an Aunt who had married and lived in Soho.
explanation: The narrator summarizes Rezia's desires and memories using her own associative logic and naive enthusiasm for English culture.
evidence: Polysyndeton ('and wanted... and the English horses, and the tailor-made suits') mimicking a stream of eager thoughts, Content is entirely restricted to Rezia's subjective desires and memories
notes: Borderline with standard free indirect discourse, but the associative chaining of 'English' items pushes it toward interior monologue.

passage: It might be possible, Septimus thought, looking at England from the train window, as they left Newhaven; it might be possible that the world itself is without meaning.
explanation: A brief intrusion of Septimus's nihilistic thought process, framed by a narratorial tag ('Septimus thought') but retaining his exact phrasing and philosophical despair.
evidence: Reporting tag ('Septimus thought') indicates narrator presence, Repetition ('It might be possible... it might be possible') mimics the cadence of his internal realization

passage: That boy’s business of the intoxication of language--_Antony and Cleopatra_--had shrivelled utterly. How Shakespeare loathed humanity--the putting on of clothes, the getting of children, the sordidity of the mouth and the belly! This was now revealed to Septimus; the message hidden in the beauty of words. The secret signal which one generation passes, under disguise, to the next is loathing, hatred, despair. Dante the same. Aeschylus (translated) the same.
explanation: The passage renders Septimus's distorted, paranoid literary interpretations as absolute facts. The narrator disappears into Septimus's absolute certainty about the 'secret signal' of despair.
evidence: Exclamatory syntax ('How Shakespeare loathed humanity--'), Fragmentary, associative conclusions ('Dante the same. Aeschylus (translated) the same.'), Presentation of subjective delusion as objective revelation

passage: Love between man and woman was repulsive to Shakespeare. The business of copulation was filth to him before the end.
explanation: These sentences are embedded in a paragraph otherwise focused on Rezia, representing a sudden, unmediated intrusion of Septimus's obsessive thoughts about Shakespeare and human reproduction.
evidence: Abrupt shift in perspective from Rezia's dialogue to Septimus's dark fixations, Absolute, declarative statements reflecting his delusion rather than narratorial truth
notes: An interesting structural choice by Woolf, dropping Septimus's dark thoughts directly into the middle of Rezia's attempts at normalcy.

passage: But nobody could be like Septimus; so gentle; so serious; so clever. Could she not read Shakespeare too? Was Shakespeare a difficult author? she asked.
explanation: Rezia's internal rationalizations and insecurities are presented through free indirect discourse that captures her pre-speech thought process before she actually asks the question aloud.
evidence: Fragmentary, affectionate listing ('so gentle; so serious; so clever'), Internal questioning ('Could she not read Shakespeare too?') leading up to spoken dialogue

passage: One cannot bring children into a world like this. One cannot perpetuate suffering, or increase the breed of these lustful animals, who have no lasting emotions, but only whims and vanities, eddying them now this way, now that.
explanation: Septimus's internal philosophical justification for refusing to have children. The use of the impersonal 'One' reflects his attempt to universalize his trauma and disgust.
evidence: Unmediated access to his philosophical reasoning, Diction reflects his misanthropy ('lustful animals', 'whims and vanities')

passage: For the truth is (let her ignore it) that human beings have neither kindness, nor faith, nor charity beyond what serves to increase the pleasure of the moment. They hunt in packs. Their packs scour the desert and vanish screaming into the wilderness. They desert the fallen. They are plastered over with grimaces. There was Brewer at the office, with his waxed moustache, coral tie-pin, white slip, and pleasurable emotions--all coldness and clamminess within,--his geraniums ruined in the War--his cook’s nerves destroyed; or Amelia What’shername, handing round cups of tea punctually at five--a leering, sneering obscene little harpy; and the Toms and Berties in their starched shirt fronts oozing thick drops of vice. They never saw him drawing pictures of them naked at their antics in his notebook. In the street, vans roared past him; brutality blared out on placards; men were trapped in mines; women burnt alive; and once a maimed file of lunatics being exercised or displayed for the diversion of the populace (who laughed aloud), ambled and nodded and grinned past him, in the Tottenham Court Road, each half apologetically, yet triumphantly, inflicting his hopeless woe. And would _he_ go mad?
explanation: A sustained dive into Septimus's paranoid consciousness. He free-associates from a general misanthropic philosophy to specific memories of his coworkers, transforming them into grotesque caricatures, before spiraling into apocalyptic visions of the street.
evidence: Associative leaps from abstract philosophy ('They hunt in packs') to specific memories ('Brewer at the office') to street scenes, Highly subjective, grotesque imagery ('leering, sneering obscene little harpy', 'oozing thick drops of vice'), Culminates in a direct, italicized internal question ('And would _he_ go mad?')
notes: A masterclass in rendering paranoid schizophrenia through modernist techniques; the external world is entirely subsumed by his terrifying internal projections.

passage: _She_ could not grow old and have no children! She was very lonely, she was very unhappy!
explanation: Rezia's desperate internal cries are rendered in the third person but carry the exact emotional weight and syntax of her own unspoken thoughts.
evidence: Italics used for emphasis on the pronoun, marking the intensity of her internal voice, Exclamatory punctuation reflecting her emotional distress, Repetition ('she was very lonely, she was very unhappy')

passage: His wife was crying, and he felt nothing; only each time she sobbed in this profound, this silent, this hopeless way, he descended another step into the pit. At last, with a melodramatic gesture which he assumed mechanically and with complete consciousness of its insincerity, he dropped his head on his hands. Now he had surrendered; now other people must help him. People must be sent for. He gave in. Nothing could rouse him.
explanation: The passage begins with the narrator describing Septimus's internal state ('descended another step into the pit', 'complete consciousness of its insincerity') but shifts into his own internal monologue of surrender ('Now he had surrendered; now other people must help him').
evidence: Psycho-narration of his emotional numbness and mechanical gestures, Shift to short, definitive sentences reflecting his internal decision to give up ('He gave in.')
notes: Hybrid passage transitioning from omniscient description of consciousness to indirect interior monologue.

passage: Oh, what a relief! What a kind man, what a good man! thought Rezia.
explanation: Direct representation of Rezia's immense relief, framed by a reporting tag but retaining the exact exclamatory nature of her thought.
evidence: Exclamatory syntax ('Oh, what a relief!'), Reporting tag ('thought Rezia')

passage: So there was no excuse; nothing whatever the matter, except the sin for which human nature had condemned him to death; that he did not feel. He had not cared when Evans was killed; that was worst; but all the other crimes raised their heads and shook their fingers and jeered and sneered over the rail of the bed in the early hours of the morning at the prostrate body which lay realising its degradation; how he had married his wife without loving her; had lied to her; seduced her; outraged Miss Isabel Pole, and was so pocked and marked with vice that women shuddered when they saw him in the street. The verdict of human nature on such a wretch was death.
explanation: Septimus's overwhelming guilt is personified ('crimes raised their heads and shook their fingers'). The narrator presents his distorted self-condemnation as the reality of his mental state.
evidence: Personification of his perceived crimes jeering at him, Listing of his supposed sins ('married his wife without loving her; had lied to her; seduced her'), Absolute, fatalistic conclusion ('The verdict of human nature... was death')

passage: Human nature, in short, was on him--the repulsive brute, with the blood-red nostrils. Holmes was on him. Dr. Holmes came quite regularly every day. Once you stumble, Septimus wrote on the back of a postcard, human nature is on you. Holmes is on you. Their only chance was to escape, without letting Holmes know; to Italy--anywhere, anywhere, away from Dr. Holmes.
explanation: Septimus conflates the abstract concept of 'human nature' with the specific figure of Dr. Holmes, viewing him as a monstrous predator. The passage renders his desperate need to flee.
evidence: Metaphorical conflation of Holmes and 'human nature' as a 'repulsive brute', Repetition of the paranoid thought ('Holmes is on you'), Frantic, associative desire for escape ('anywhere, anywhere, away from Dr. Holmes')

passage: Dr. Holmes was such a kind man. He was so interested in Septimus. He only wanted to help them, he said.
explanation: Rezia's naive internal rationalization and trust in Dr. Holmes, contrasting sharply with Septimus's terror of him in the preceding sentences.
evidence: Simple, declarative sentences reflecting her earnest belief, Adoption of Holmes's own justifications ('He only wanted to help them')

passage: So he was deserted. The whole world was clamouring: Kill yourself, kill yourself, for our sakes. But why should he kill himself for their sakes? Food was pleasant; the sun hot; and this killing oneself, how does one set about it, with a table knife, uglily, with floods of blood,--by sucking a gaspipe? He was too weak; he could scarcely raise his hand. Besides, now that he was quite alone, condemned, deserted, as those who are about to die are alone, there was a luxury in it, an isolation full of sublimity; a freedom which the attached can never know. Holmes had won of course; the brute with the red nostrils had won. But even Holmes himself could not touch this last relic straying on the edge of the world, this outcast, who gazed back at the inhabited regions, who lay, like a drowned sailor, on the shore of the world.
explanation: A profound dive into Septimus's suicidal ideation and subsequent feeling of sublime isolation. The narrative voice completely adopts his logic, his auditory hallucinations, and his self-mythologizing imagery.
evidence: Integration of auditory hallucination ('The whole world was clamouring: Kill yourself...'), Practical, detached consideration of suicide methods ('with a table knife, uglily... by sucking a gaspipe?'), Extended poetic metaphor of himself as a 'drowned sailor, on the shore of the world'

passage: It was at that moment (Rezia gone shopping) that the great revelation took place. A voice spoke from behind the screen. Evans was speaking. The dead were with him.
explanation: The narrator presents Septimus's hallucination as an objective event ('A voice spoke... Evans was speaking'), perfectly mirroring how real and undeniable the delusion is to Septimus's consciousness.
evidence: Hallucination presented as factual narrative reality, Short, definitive sentences marking the onset of the delusion

passage: So there was a man outside; Evans presumably; and the roses, which Rezia said were half dead, had been picked by him in the fields of Greece.
explanation: Septimus internally processes Rezia's actions (bringing in roses) through the lens of his active hallucination, logically concluding that his dead friend Evans brought them from Greece.
evidence: Internal logical deduction ('So there was a man outside; Evans presumably'), Integration of external reality (the roses) into his internal delusion (picked in Greece)

passage: It was precisely twelve o’clock; twelve by Big Ben; whose stroke was wafted over the northern part of London; blent with that of other clocks, mixed in a thin ethereal way with the clouds and wisps of smoke, and died up there among the seagulls--twelve o’clock struck as Clarissa Dalloway laid her green dress on her bed, and the Warren Smiths walked down Harley Street.
explanation: A classic example of Woolf's space-montage technique. The external sound of Big Ben striking twelve is used as a cinematic transition to cut across physical space, linking Clarissa Dalloway's actions simultaneously with the Warren Smiths' walk.
evidence: Use of an external auditory stimulus (Big Ben) to traverse space, Simultaneous juxtaposition of two separate character groups ('Clarissa Dalloway laid her green dress... and the Warren Smiths walked'), Cinematic, sweeping narratorial perspective ('wafted over the northern part of London')
notes: This is a structural device organizing the presentation of consciousness across the novel, rather than a direct representation of a single mind.

passage: Probably, Rezia thought, that was Sir William Bradshaw’s house with the grey motor car in front of it.
explanation: A brief, tagged thought from Rezia as she observes their surroundings, rendered in her own speculative voice.
evidence: Speculative diction ('Probably'), Reporting tag ('Rezia thought')

passage: Probably, Rezia thought, that was Sir William Bradshaw’s house with the grey motor car in front of it.
explanation: The narrator frames the thought with 'Rezia thought', but the speculative 'Probably' and the immediate observation belong to Rezia's internal voice.
evidence: Use of the character's speculative diction ('Probably'), Reporting tag ('Rezia thought') embedded within the thought

passage: thinking sometimes of the patient, sometimes, excusably, of the wall of gold, mounting minute by minute while she waited; the wall of gold that was mounting between them and all shifts and anxieties (she had borne them bravely; they had had their struggles) until she felt wedged on a calm ocean, where only spice winds blow; respected, admired, envied, with scarcely anything left to wish for, though she regretted her stoutness; large dinner-parties every Thursday night to the profession; an occasional bazaar to be opened; Royalty greeted; too little time, alas, with her husband, whose work grew and grew; a boy doing well at Eton; she would have liked a daughter too; interests she had, however, in plenty; child welfare; the after-care of the epileptic, and photography
explanation: The passage begins as omniscient description ('thinking sometimes of...') but quickly dissolves into a stream of Lady Bradshaw's associative thoughts, listing her comforts, regrets, and interests in a fragmented, paratactic style that mimics her wandering mind.
evidence: Fragmented, list-like syntax ('large dinner-parties... a boy doing well at Eton; she would have liked a daughter too'), Parenthetical intrusion of her own self-justification ('she had borne them bravely; they had had their struggles'), Associative drift from wealth to social duties to family to hobbies
notes: A classic example of narrator-mediated text sliding into free indirect discourse/indirect interior monologue.

passage: Ah yes (those general practitioners! thought Sir William. It took half his time to undo their blunders. Some were irreparable).
explanation: Sir William's arrogant, dismissive thoughts about general practitioners are presented directly in his idiom, enclosed in parentheses to mark them as a private mental aside during his consultation.
evidence: Parentheses used to visually separate the internal thought from the external action, Exclamatory, judgmental diction ('those general practitioners!', 'blunders'), Reporting tag ('thought Sir William') embedded inside the parentheses

passage: The European War--that little shindy of schoolboys with gunpowder? Had he served with distinction? He really forgot. In the War itself he had failed.
explanation: Septimus internally processes Sir William's question about the war. The cynical redefinition of the war and the self-questioning are rendered in his own disjointed, traumatized voice, despite the third-person pronouns.
evidence: Rhetorical self-questioning ('Had he served with distinction?'), Highly subjective, cynical metaphor ('that little shindy of schoolboys with gunpowder'), Third-person pronouns ('he') combined with character-specific thought content

passage: He had committed an appalling crime and been condemned to death by human nature.
explanation: This sentence reflects Septimus's internal delusion and paranoia, presented as a factual statement within the narrative flow, characteristic of free indirect discourse rendering a disturbed consciousness.
evidence: Delusional content presented as objective narrative fact, Absence of reporting verbs (no 'he thought that')
notes: Could also be classified as omniscient description of consciousness, but the phrasing 'condemned to death by human nature' is a specific recurring motif in Septimus's own mind.

passage: The fellow made a distasteful impression.
explanation: A brief flash of Septimus's subjective judgment of Sir William, rendered in third person but reflecting Septimus's immediate internal reaction.
evidence: Subjective judgment ('distasteful impression'), Immediate reaction to the preceding dialogue

passage: Once you fall, Septimus repeated to himself, human nature is on you. Holmes and Bradshaw are on you. They scour the desert. They fly screaming into the wilderness. The rack and the thumbscrew are applied. Human nature is remorseless.
explanation: Septimus is actively 'repeating to himself' a highly rhetorical, organized, and metaphorical argument about his persecution. The structure is more coherent and argumentative than raw interior monologue.
evidence: Explicit self-address ('repeated to himself'), Sustained, escalating metaphorical imagery ('scour the desert', 'rack and the thumbscrew'), Rhetorical, declarative syntax

passage: But if he confessed? If he communicated? Would they let him off then, his torturers?
explanation: A rapid series of panicked internal questions. The staccato syntax simulates his rising anxiety and desperation.
evidence: Consecutive unanswered questions, Paranoid diction ('his torturers'), Fragmented, urgent rhythm

passage: But what was his crime? He could not remember it.
explanation: Septimus's internal confusion rendered in free indirect discourse.
evidence: Direct internal question ('But what was his crime?'), Immediate internal answer/realization ('He could not remember it')

passage: (But it was growing late.)
explanation: A parenthetical intrusion representing Sir William's private, impatient thought interrupting his outward display of encouragement.
evidence: Parentheses marking a shift to private thought, Contrast between outward dialogue ('Yes? Sir William encouraged him') and internal impatience

passage: Love, trees, there is no crime--what was his message? He could not remember it.
explanation: Septimus's fragmented attempt to recall his 'message' to the world. The associative leap from 'Love' to 'trees' to 'no crime' mimics his disordered thought process.
evidence: Asyntactic list of concepts ('Love, trees, there is no crime'), Internal self-questioning ('what was his message?')

passage: Really, he was not fit to be about.
explanation: Sir William's private, clinical judgment of Septimus, rendered in free indirect discourse immediately following his spoken dialogue.
evidence: Judgmental, dismissive tone ('Really, he was not fit'), Third-person pronoun ('he') referring to Septimus from Sir William's perspective

passage: Never, never had Rezia felt such agony in her life! She had asked for help and been deserted! He had failed them! Sir William Bradshaw was not a nice man.
explanation: Rezia's internal panic and despair are rendered through exclamatory free indirect discourse. The repetition and exclamation marks simulate her acute emotional distress.
evidence: Exclamatory punctuation, Repetition for emotional emphasis ('Never, never'), Childlike, absolute judgment ('was not a nice man') reflecting her raw emotional state

passage: They had been deserted. But what more did she want?
explanation: A continuation of Rezia's internal despair, featuring a rhetorical question she asks herself.
evidence: Internal rhetorical question, Continuation of the 'deserted' motif from her previous thoughts

passage: subconsciously one was grateful to Rigby and Lowndes for giving one time ratified by Greenwich; and this gratitude (so Hugh Whitbread ruminated, dallying there in front of the shop window), naturally took the form later of buying off Rigby and Lowndes socks or shoes.
explanation: The narrator explicitly describes Hugh Whitbread's subconscious mental processes and rationalizations, maintaining a slightly ironic authorial distance.
evidence: Explicit narratorial framing ('so Hugh Whitbread ruminated'), Use of the generalized pronoun 'one', Analysis of a 'subconscious' feeling

passage: Lady Bruton preferred Richard Dalloway of course. He was made of much finer material. But she wouldn’t let them run down her poor dear Hugh. She could never forget his kindness--he had been really remarkably kind--she forgot precisely upon what occasion. But he had been--remarkably kind. Anyhow, the difference between one man and another does not amount to much. She had never seen the sense of cutting people up, as Clarissa Dalloway did--cutting them up and sticking them together again; not at any rate when one was sixty-two.
explanation: The narrative slips entirely into Lady Bruton's idiom and thought process. The text tracks her associative memory failure ('she forgot precisely upon what occasion') and her subsequent rationalization.
evidence: Character-specific idioms ('poor dear Hugh', 'cutting people up'), Self-correction and memory lapses rendered in real-time ('she forgot precisely... But he had been'), Rationalizing transitions ('Anyhow, the difference...')

passage: She thought him an underbred fellow.
explanation: While structurally resembling psycho-narration, the phrase 'underbred fellow' is Miss Brush's specific, snobbish internal judgment rendered as free indirect discourse.
evidence: Character-specific judgmental diction ('underbred fellow'), Immediate internal reaction to Hugh's spoken dialogue
notes: Borderline with omniscient description, but the diction tips it toward indirect interior monologue.

passage: Which was she now, the General’s great-grand-daughter? great-great-grand-daughter? Richard Dalloway asked himself.
explanation: Richard Dalloway's internal question is presented directly, followed by a reporting tag.
evidence: Direct internal question, Reporting tag ('asked himself')

passage: Which was she now, the General’s great-grand-daughter? great-great-grand-daughter? Richard Dalloway asked himself. Sir Roderick, Sir Miles, Sir Talbot--that was it. It was remarkable how in that family the likeness persisted in the women. She should have been a general of dragoons herself. And Richard would have served under her, cheerfully; he had the greatest respect for her; he cherished these romantic views about well-set-up old women of pedigree, and would have liked, in his good-humoured way, to bring some young hot-heads of his acquaintance to lunch with her; as if a type like hers could be bred of amiable tea-drinking enthusiasts! He knew her country. He knew her people.
explanation: The passage begins with a direct question framed by the narrator ('Richard Dalloway asked himself'), but quickly slips into Richard's unmediated associative thought process ('Sir Roderick, Sir Miles, Sir Talbot--that was it'). The exclamation ('as if a type like hers could be bred...') reflects his specific idiom and emotional inflection, characteristic of free indirect discourse rendering pre-speech thought.
evidence: Fragmentary associative recall ('Sir Roderick, Sir Miles, Sir Talbot--that was it.'), Exclamatory syntax reflecting character idiom ('as if a type like hers could be bred of amiable tea-drinking enthusiasts!'), Third-person pronouns maintained while adopting the character's subjective viewpoint.
notes: A classic example of Woolf's fluid transition from psycho-narration into indirect interior monologue.

passage: Peter Walsh! All three, Lady Bruton, Hugh Whitbread, and Richard Dalloway, remembered the same thing--how passionately Peter had been in love; been rejected; gone to India; come a cropper; made a mess of things; and Richard Dalloway had a very great liking for the dear old fellow too.
character_pov: Collective (Lady Bruton, Hugh Whitbread, Richard Dalloway)
explanation: The narrator summarizes a collective memory, but the diction ('come a cropper; made a mess of things') belongs to the characters' shared social idiom rather than the narrator's formal voice. The exclamation 'Peter Walsh!' mimics the spontaneous spark of recognition in their minds.
evidence: Exclamatory thought intrusion ('Peter Walsh!'), Colloquial phrasing reflecting the characters' class and idiom ('come a cropper; made a mess of things')
notes: This is a boundary case of collective free indirect discourse used to represent a shared mental state.

passage: for what was he thinking, she wondered, about Peter Walsh? That Peter Walsh had been in love with Clarissa; that he would go back directly after lunch and find Clarissa; that he would tell her, in so many words, that he loved her. Yes, he would say that. Milly Brush once might almost have fallen in love with these silences; and Mr. Dalloway was always so dependable; such a gentleman too.
explanation: The narrator frames the thought ('she wondered'), but the subsequent sentences are Milly Brush's internal deductions and evaluations. The affirmative self-answer ('Yes, he would say that') and the concluding assessment ('such a gentleman too') are direct representations of her inner voice in the third person.
evidence: Self-answering internal dialogue ('Yes, he would say that.'), Character-specific evaluative diction ('such a gentleman too.')

passage: Hugh was very slow, Lady Bruton thought. He was getting fat, she noticed. Richard always kept himself in the pink of condition. She was getting impatient; the whole of her being was setting positively, undeniably, domineeringly brushing aside all this unnecessary trifling (Peter Walsh and his affairs) upon that subject which engaged her attention...
explanation: The passage blends narrator reporting ('Lady Bruton thought', 'she noticed') with Lady Bruton's subjective judgments ('getting fat', 'in the pink of condition'). The narrator then takes over to describe her mounting impatience, making this a hybrid of indirect interior monologue and omniscient description of consciousness.
evidence: Subjective idioms embedded in narrative ('in the pink of condition'), Narrator framing of internal observations ('she noticed')
notes: Transitions smoothly from FID into omniscient psycho-narration.

passage: Always she went back to those fields down in Devonshire, where she had jumped the brooks on Patty, her pony, with Mortimer and Tom, her brothers. And there were the dogs; there were the rats; there were her father and mother on the lawn under the trees, with the tea-things out, and the beds of dahlias, the hollyhocks, the pampas grass; and they, little wretches, always up to some mischief! stealing back through the shrubbery, so as not to be seen, all bedraggled from some roguery. What old nurse used to say about her frocks! Ah dear, she remembered--it was Wednesday in Brook Street.
explanation: As Lady Bruton dozes, her mind drifts associatively through childhood memories. The syntax becomes exclamatory and fragmented ('and they, little wretches, always up to some mischief!', 'What old nurse used to say about her frocks!'), perfectly capturing the unedited flow of nostalgic recall before she abruptly returns to the present ('Ah dear, she remembered--it was Wednesday...').
evidence: Exclamatory, unmediated memory fragments ('little wretches, always up to some mischief!'), Associative linking of sensory details (dogs, rats, lawn, tea-things, dahlias), Abrupt temporal shift back to the present ('Ah dear, she remembered...')

passage: Murmuring London flowed up to her, and her hand, lying on the sofa back, curled upon some imaginary baton such as her grandfathers might have held, holding which she seemed, drowsy and heavy, to be commanding battalions marching to Canada, and those good fellows walking across London, that territory of theirs, that little bit of carpet, Mayfair. And they went further and further from her, being attached to her by a thin thread (since they had lunched with her) which would stretch and stretch, get thinner and thinner as they walked across London; as if one’s friends were attached to one’s body, after lunching with them, by a thin thread, which (as she dozed there) became hazy with the sound of bells, striking the hour or ringing to service, as a single spider’s thread is blotted with rain-drops, and, burdened, sags down.
explanation: The narrator describes Lady Bruton's half-asleep, dream-like state using elaborate metaphors (the imaginary baton, the spider's thread). While it represents her consciousness and physical sensations of dozing, the highly crafted, poetic language belongs entirely to the narrator, not the character.
evidence: Elaborate narratorial metaphors ('single spider's thread is blotted with rain-drops'), Description of physical actions linked to mental states ('curled upon some imaginary baton'), Third-person framing of a dream-like state ('she seemed, drowsy and heavy, to be commanding')
notes: A prime example of Woolf's use of omniscient description and literary impressionism to render the feeling of falling asleep.

passage: In Norfolk, of which Richard Dalloway was half thinking, a soft warm wind blew back the petals; confused the waters; ruffled the flowering grasses. Haymakers, who had pitched beneath hedges to sleep away the morning toil, parted curtains of green blades; moved trembling globes of cow parsley to see the sky; the blue, the steadfast, the blazing summer sky.
explanation: The narrator explicitly states Richard is 'half thinking' of Norfolk, and then provides a lush, poetic description of the landscape. The imagery represents the content of his reverie, but the lyrical articulation is entirely the narrator's.
evidence: Explicit narrator framing ('of which Richard Dalloway was half thinking'), Highly polished, poetic sensory description ('trembling globes of cow parsley')
notes: Demonstrates how Woolf uses narrator-controlled imagery to represent the background hum of a character's mind.

passage: Life had thrown up this wreckage; shop windows full of coloured paste, and one stood stark with the lethargy of the old, stiff with the rigidity of the old, looking in. Evelyn Whitbread might like to buy this Spanish necklace--so she might. Yawn he must.
explanation: Richard's torpid mental state is rendered through his own cynical observations ('Life had thrown up this wreckage') and disjointed thoughts about Hugh's wife ('so she might'). The final phrase ('Yawn he must') is a direct internal imperative rendered in the third person.
evidence: Subjective, cynical metaphor ('wreckage', 'coloured paste'), Internal self-command/observation ('Yawn he must.')

passage: Goodness knows he didn’t want to go buying necklaces with Hugh. But there are tides in the body. Morning meets afternoon. Borne like a frail shallop on deep, deep floods, Lady Bruton’s great-grandfather and his memoir and his campaigns in North America were whelmed and sunk. And Millicent Bruton too. She went under. Richard didn’t care a straw what became of Emigration; about that letter, whether the editor put it in or not. The necklace hung stretched between Hugh’s admirable fingers. Let him give it to a girl, if he must buy jewels--any girl, any girl in the street. For the worthlessness of this life did strike Richard pretty forcibly--buying necklaces for Evelyn. If he’d had a boy he’d have said, Work, work. But he had his Elizabeth; he adored his Elizabeth.
explanation: The passage flows through Richard's dismissive thoughts about Lady Bruton's project, his irritation with Hugh, and his sudden philosophical reflection on life's worthlessness, culminating in a thought about his daughter. The transitions are associative and the diction ('didn't care a straw', 'Work, work') is entirely his own.
evidence: Colloquial character idiom ('Goodness knows', 'didn't care a straw'), Associative leaps from the immediate scene (the necklace) to abstract thoughts (worthlessness of life) to his daughter., Internal imperatives ('Let him give it to a girl...')

passage: For he never gave Clarissa presents, except a bracelet two or three years ago, which had not been a success. She never wore it. It pained him to remember that she never wore it. And as a single spider’s thread after wavering here and there attaches itself to the point of a leaf, so Richard’s mind, recovering from its lethargy, set now on his wife, Clarissa, whom Peter Walsh had loved so passionately; and Richard had had a sudden vision of her there at luncheon; of himself and Clarissa; of their life together; and he drew the tray of old jewels towards him, and taking up first this brooch then that ring, “How much is that?” he asked, but doubted his own taste. He wanted to open the drawing-room door and come in holding out something; a present for Clarissa. Only what?
explanation: The text moves from Richard's painful memory of the bracelet into a narrator-guided metaphor (the spider's thread), then back into Richard's associative thoughts about Clarissa and Peter Walsh. It ends with a direct, unmediated internal question ('Only what?').
evidence: Repetitive thought patterns indicating emotional pain ('She never wore it. It pained him to remember that she never wore it.'), Unmediated internal question ('Only what?')
notes: A hybrid passage where the narrator steps in to describe the mechanics of Richard's thought process (the spider thread metaphor) before yielding back to his internal voice.

passage: He was unspeakably pompous. Really, after dealing here for thirty-five years he was not going to be put off by a mere boy who did not know his business. For Dubonnet, it seemed, was out, and Hugh would not buy anything until Mr. Dubonnet chose to be in; at which the youth flushed and bowed his correct little bow. It was all perfectly correct. And yet Richard couldn’t have said that to save his life! Why these people stood that damned insolence he could not conceive. Hugh was becoming an intolerable ass. Richard Dalloway could not stand more than an hour of his society.
explanation: Richard's irritation with Hugh is expressed through free indirect discourse. The judgments ('unspeakably pompous', 'intolerable ass') and the exclamatory frustration ('couldn't have said that to save his life!') are direct representations of his inner monologue, maintaining third-person pronouns.
evidence: Strong evaluative language belonging to the character ('unspeakably pompous', 'damned insolence', 'intolerable ass'), Exclamatory syntax reflecting internal frustration.

passage: Flowers? Yes, flowers, since he did not trust his taste in gold; any number of flowers, roses, orchids, to celebrate what was, reckoning things as you will, an event; this feeling about her when they spoke of Peter Walsh at luncheon; and they never spoke of it; not for years had they spoken of it; which, he thought, grasping his red and white roses together (a vast bunch in tissue paper), is the greatest mistake in the world. The time comes when it can’t be said; one’s too shy to say it, he thought, pocketing his sixpence or two of change, setting off with his great bunch held against his body to Westminster to say straight out in so many words (whatever she might think of him), holding out his flowers, “I love you.” Why not? Really it was a miracle thinking of the war, and thousands of poor chaps, with all their lives before them, shovelled together, already half forgotten; it was a miracle. Here he was walking across London to say to Clarissa in so many words that he loved her. Which one never does say, he thought. Partly one’s lazy; partly one’s shy. And Clarissa--it was difficult to think of her; except in starts, as at luncheon, when he saw her quite distinctly; their whole life.
explanation: The passage is a deep dive into Richard's consciousness as he walks. It features direct internal questions and answers ('Flowers? Yes, flowers...', 'Why not?'), associative leaps from his marriage to the war ('thousands of poor chaps... shovelled together'), and fragmented reflections on his own emotional reticence ('Partly one's lazy; partly one's shy'). The narrator's presence is reduced to occasional 'he thought' tags.
evidence: Direct internal questions and answers ('Flowers? Yes, flowers...', 'Why not?'), Associative linking of personal emotion to broader historical trauma (the war), Fragmented, self-analytical syntax ('Partly one's lazy; partly one's shy.')
notes: A masterful blend of direct and indirect interior monologue, showing Richard actively composing his thoughts and intentions.

passage: The time comes when it can’t be said; one’s too shy to say it, he thought, pocketing his sixpence or two of change, setting off with his great bunch held against his body to Westminster to say straight out in so many words (whatever she might think of him), holding out his flowers, “I love you.” Why not? Really it was a miracle thinking of the war, and thousands of poor chaps, with all their lives before them, shovelled together, already half forgotten; it was a miracle. Here he was walking across London to say to Clarissa in so many words that he loved her. Which one never does say, he thought. Partly one’s lazy; partly one’s shy. And Clarissa--it was difficult to think of her; except in starts, as at luncheon, when he saw her quite distinctly; their whole life.
explanation: The passage renders Richard's unspoken thoughts using third-person pronouns and past tense, maintaining a light narratorial frame ('he thought'). The associative leap from his personal shyness to the war dead demonstrates free association within the monologue.
evidence: Use of third-person pronouns ('he thought', 'his life') mixed with character-specific idiom ('poor chaps', 'shovelled together'), Rhetorical questions addressed to himself ('Why not?'), Associative drift from the flowers to the war to Clarissa

passage: But it did make his blood boil to see little creatures of five or six crossing Piccadilly alone. The police ought to have stopped the traffic at once. He had no illusions about the London police. Indeed, he was collecting evidence of their malpractices; and those costermongers, not allowed to stand their barrows in the streets; and prostitutes, good Lord, the fault wasn’t in them, nor in young men either, but in our detestable social system and so forth; all of which he considered, could be seen considering, grey, dogged, dapper, clean, as he walked across the Park to tell his wife that he loved her.
explanation: The text presents Richard's internal indignation about social issues. The narrator is clearly present, framing the thoughts at the end ('all of which he considered, could be seen considering'), but the diction ('good Lord', 'our detestable social system') belongs entirely to Richard's consciousness.
evidence: Exclamatory interjections ('good Lord'), Character-specific political rhetoric ('our detestable social system and so forth'), Explicit narratorial framing at the end of the sentence
notes: A classic example of free indirect discourse functioning as indirect interior monologue, with the narrator stepping back in to reassert external description at the end.

passage: As for Buckingham Palace (like an old prima donna facing the audience all in white) you can’t deny it a certain dignity, he considered, nor despise what does, after all, stand to millions of people (a little crowd was waiting at the gate to see the King drive out) for a symbol, absurd though it is; a child with a box of bricks could have done better, he thought; looking at the memorial to Queen Victoria (whom he could remember in her horn spectacles driving through Kensington), its white mound, its billowing motherliness; but he liked being ruled by the descendant of Horsa; he liked continuity; and the sense of handing on the traditions of the past. It was a great age in which to have lived. Indeed, his own life was a miracle; let him make no mistake about it; here he was, in the prime of life, walking to his house in Westminster to tell Clarissa that he loved her. Happiness is this he thought.
explanation: Richard's visual perceptions trigger associative thoughts about history, monarchy, and his own life. The narrator uses 'he considered' and 'he thought', but the syntax is loose and associative, mimicking the flow of his mind as he walks.
evidence: Parenthetical intrusions representing simultaneous perception or memory ('a little crowd was waiting...', 'whom he could remember...'), Direct address to an implied listener or self ('you can't deny it', 'let him make no mistake about it'), Concluding with a direct, unmediated thought ('Happiness is this he thought')

passage: It was perfectly true that she had not asked Ellie Henderson to her party; but she had done it on purpose. Now Mrs. Marsham wrote “she had told Ellie Henderson she would ask Clarissa--Ellie so much wanted to come.” But why should she invite all the dull women in London to her parties? Why should Mrs. Marsham interfere? And there was Elizabeth closeted all this time with Doris Kilman. Anything more nauseating she could not conceive. Prayer at this hour with that woman.
explanation: The narrative drops directly into Clarissa's annoyed thoughts at her writing table. The questions are rhetorical and internal, and the fragmented syntax at the end ('Prayer at this hour with that woman.') reflects her disgust.
evidence: Internal rhetorical questions ('But why should she invite...', 'Why should Mrs. Marsham interfere?'), Fragmented, verbless concluding sentence mimicking a disgusted thought ('Prayer at this hour with that woman.'), Highly subjective, emotional diction ('nauseating', 'dull women')

passage: Who at this hour? Three, good Heavens! Three already! For with overpowering directness and dignity the clock struck three; and she heard nothing else; but the door handle slipped round and in came Richard! What a surprise! In came Richard, holding out flowers. She had failed him, once at Constantinople; and Lady Bruton, whose lunch parties were said to be extraordinarily amusing, had not asked her. He was holding out flowers--roses, red and white roses. (But he could not bring himself to say he loved her; not in so many words.)
explanation: The passage captures Clarissa's immediate, startled reactions to the door opening, followed by a rapid associative memory of her failures. It concludes with a parenthetical shift into Richard's consciousness.
evidence: Exclamatory, fragmented thoughts ('Who at this hour? Three, good Heavens!'), Associative leap from seeing Richard to remembering her failures ('She had failed him, once at Constantinople'), Parentheses used to mark a sudden shift into another character's mind ('But he could not bring himself...')
notes: The parenthetical sentence at the end is a classic Woolfian space-montage technique, instantly cutting from Clarissa's perception of the flowers to Richard's internal inability to speak.

passage: And was it amusing, she asked? Had Lady Bruton asked after her? Peter Walsh was back. Mrs. Marsham had written. Must she ask Ellie Henderson? That woman Kilman was upstairs.
explanation: While Clarissa is speaking aloud ('she asked'), her internal anxieties are running simultaneously in a rapid-fire list of disconnected worries.
evidence: Staccato, disconnected sentences reflecting a racing mind, Juxtaposition of spoken dialogue tags ('she asked') with purely internal worries ('Must she ask Ellie Henderson?')

passage: What had they been doing? Oh, it was for the party; no, he had not forgotten, the party. Peter Walsh was back. Oh yes; she had had him. And he was going to get a divorce; and he was in love with some woman out there. And he hadn’t changed in the slightest. There she was, mending her dress....
explanation: Richard's internal processing of the room's appearance and Clarissa's news about Peter Walsh. The self-correction ('Oh, it was for the party; no, he had not forgotten') perfectly mimics the real-time process of realization.
evidence: Self-answering internal questions ('What had they been doing? Oh, it was for the party'), Conversational internal filler ('Oh yes'), Trailing off into observation ('There she was, mending her dress....')

passage: She had met him too! Well, he was getting absolutely intolerable. Buying Evelyn necklaces; fatter than ever; an intolerable ass.
explanation: Clarissa's unmediated, highly judgmental thoughts about Hugh Whitbread, triggered by Richard mentioning him.
evidence: Fragmented syntax ('Buying Evelyn necklaces; fatter than ever'), Strong subjective judgment ('an intolerable ass'), Exclamatory internal reaction ('She had met him too!')

passage: (But he could not tell her he loved her. He held her hand. Happiness is this, he thought.)
explanation: A brief intrusion into Richard's mind, marked by parentheses, contrasting his internal feeling of happiness with his external inability to speak.
evidence: Parenthetical enclosure separating the thought from the surrounding dialogue, Direct thought tag ('Happiness is this, he thought')

passage: --it was a very odd thing how much Clarissa minded about her parties, he thought. But Richard had no notion of the look of a room. However--what was he going to say? If she worried about these parties he would not let her give them. Did she wish she had married Peter? But he must go. He must be off, he said, getting up. But he stood for a moment as if he were about to say something; and she wondered what? Why? There were the roses.
explanation: The passage begins in Richard's mind as he considers Clarissa's anxiety, then seamlessly shifts mid-paragraph into Clarissa's mind as she observes him standing there.
evidence: Internal questioning by Richard ('However--what was he going to say? Did she wish she had married Peter?'), Mid-paragraph perspective shift to Clarissa ('and she wondered what? Why? There were the roses.')
notes: A prime example of Woolf's fluid space-montage technique, passing the narrative baton from one consciousness to another within a single continuous scene.

passage: And there is a dignity in people; a solitude; even between husband and wife a gulf; and that one must respect, thought Clarissa, watching him open the door; for one would not part with it oneself, or take it, against his will, from one’s husband, without losing one’s independence, one’s self-respect--something, after all, priceless.
explanation: Clarissa's philosophical reflection on marriage and independence, triggered by Richard's departure. The thought is rendered in her characteristic sweeping, generalized idiom ('one would not part with it oneself').
evidence: Thought tag ('thought Clarissa'), Use of the generalized 'one' reflecting her internal rationalization, Abstract, philosophical content arising from a mundane action (watching him open the door)

passage: How like him! He would go on saying “An hour’s complete rest after luncheon” to the end of time, because a doctor had ordered it once. It was like him to take what doctors said literally; part of his adorable, divine simplicity, which no one had to the same extent; which made him go and do the thing while she and Peter frittered their time away bickering.
explanation: Clarissa's affectionate, slightly exasperated thoughts about Richard's literal-mindedness.
evidence: Exclamatory opening ('How like him!'), Subjective evaluation ('adorable, divine simplicity'), Comparison to her own past ('while she and Peter frittered their time away')

passage: But--but--why did she suddenly feel, for no reason that she could discover, desperately unhappy? As a person who has dropped some grain of pearl or diamond into the grass and parts the tall blades very carefully, this way and that, and searches here and there vainly, and at last spies it there at the roots, so she went through one thing and another; no, it was not Sally Seton saying that Richard would never be in the Cabinet because he had a second-class brain (it came back to her); no, she did not mind that; nor was it to do with Elizabeth either and Doris Kilman; those were facts. It was a feeling, some unpleasant feeling, earlier in the day perhaps; something that Peter had said, combined with some depression of her own, in her bedroom, taking off her hat; and what Richard had said had added to it, but what had he said? There were his roses. Her parties! That was it! Her parties! Both of them criticised her very unfairly, laughed at her very unjustly, for her parties. That was it! That was it! Well, how was she going to defend herself? Now that she knew what it was, she felt perfectly happy.
explanation: A brilliant simulation of a mind frantically searching for the source of its own anxiety. The syntax mimics the physical act of searching described in the simile, discarding wrong answers until hitting upon the realization.
evidence: Stuttering start ('But--but--why did she suddenly feel...'), Process of elimination mimicking mental searching ('no, it was not Sally Seton... no, she did not mind that; nor was it to do with Elizabeth'), Sudden exclamatory realization ('Her parties! That was it! Her parties!')
notes: The extended simile of searching for a pearl in the grass is narratorial, but it perfectly structures the subsequent interior monologue, making this a highly integrated hybrid of omniscient description and indirect interior monologue.

passage: But suppose Peter said to her, “Yes, yes, but your parties--what’s the sense of your parties?” all she could say was (and nobody could be expected to understand): They’re an offering; which sounded horribly vague. But who was Peter to make out that life was all plain sailing?--Peter always in love, always in love with the wrong woman? What’s your love? she might say to him. And she knew his answer; how it is the most important thing in the world and no woman possibly understood it. Very well. But could any man understand what she meant either? about life? She could not imagine Peter or Richard taking the trouble to give a party for no reason whatever. But to go deeper, beneath what people said (and these judgements, how superficial, how fragmentary they are!) in her own mind now, what did it mean to her, this thing she called life? Oh, it was very queer. Here was So-and-so in South Kensington; some one up in Bayswater; and somebody else, say, in Mayfair. And she felt quite continuously a sense of their existence; and she felt what a waste; and she felt what a pity; and she felt if only they could be brought together; so she did it. And it was an offering; to combine, to create; but to whom? An offering for the sake of offering, perhaps. Anyhow, it was her gift. Nothing else had she of the slightest importance; could not think, write, even play the piano. She muddled Armenians and Turks; loved success; hated discomfort; must be liked; talked oceans of nonsense: and to this day, ask her what the Equator was, and she did not know. All the same, that one day should follow another; Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday; that one should wake up in the morning; see the sky; walk in the park; meet Hugh Whitbread; then suddenly in came Peter; then these roses; it was enough. After that, how unbelievable death was!--that it must end; and no one in the whole world would know how she had loved it all; how, every instant....
explanation: Clarissa engages in a sustained internal reverie, constructing an imaginary argument with Peter Walsh to justify her parties, which evolves into a profound philosophical reflection on her love of life and fear of death.
evidence: Constructed imaginary dialogue ('But suppose Peter said to her...', 'What's your love? she might say to him'), Self-deprecating internal inventory ('could not think, write, even play the piano. She muddled Armenians and Turks'), Trailing off into the overwhelming thought of death ('how unbelievable death was!... how, every instant....')

passage: Was it that some Mongol had been wrecked on the coast of Norfolk (as Mrs. Hilbery said), had mixed with the Dalloway ladies, perhaps, a hundred years ago? For the Dalloways, in general, were fair-haired; blue-eyed; Elizabeth, on the contrary, was dark; had Chinese eyes in a pale face; an Oriental mystery; was gentle, considerate, still. As a child, she had had a perfect sense of humour; but now at seventeen, why, Clarissa could not in the least understand, she had become very serious; like a hyacinth, sheathed in glossy green, with buds just tinted, a hyacinth which has had no sun.
explanation: Clarissa's thoughts upon seeing Elizabeth enter the room. The passage blends her internal questioning about Elizabeth's appearance with a highly poetic, narrator-assisted image of the hyacinth.
evidence: Internal questioning ('Was it that some Mongol had been wrecked...'), Subjective bewilderment ('why, Clarissa could not in the least understand'), Sustained poetic imagery reflecting Clarissa's perception ('like a hyacinth, sheathed in glossy green')
notes: The hyacinth metaphor leans toward omniscient description of consciousness, but it is anchored in Clarissa's immediate perception of her daughter.

passage: Yes, Miss Kilman stood on the landing, and wore a mackintosh; but had her reasons. First, it was cheap; second, she was over forty; and did not, after all, dress to please. She was poor, moreover; degradingly poor. Otherwise she would not be taking jobs from people like the Dalloways; from rich people, who liked to be kind. Mr. Dalloway, to do him justice, had been kind. But Mrs. Dalloway had not. She had been merely condescending. She came from the most worthless of all classes--the rich, with a smattering of culture. They had expensive things everywhere; pictures, carpets, lots of servants. She considered that she had a perfect right to anything that the Dalloways did for her. She had been cheated. Yes, the word was no exaggeration, for surely a girl has a right to some kind of happiness? And she had never been happy, what with being so clumsy and so poor. And then, just as she might have had a chance at Miss Dolby’s school, the war came; and she had never been able to tell lies. Miss Dolby thought she would be happier with people who shared her views about the Germans. She had had to go. It was true that the family was of German origin; spelt the name Kiehlman in the eighteenth century; but her brother had been killed. They turned her out because she would not pretend that the Germans were all villains--when she had German friends, when the only happy days of her life had been spent in Germany! And after all, she could read history. She had had to take whatever she could get. Mr. Dalloway had come across her working for the Friends. He had allowed her (and that was really generous of him) to teach his daughter history. Also she did a little Extension lecturing and so on. Then Our Lord had come to her (and here she always bowed her head). She had seen the light two years and three months ago. Now she did not envy women like Clarissa Dalloway; she pitied them. She pitied and despised them from the bottom of her heart, as she stood on the soft carpet, looking at the old engraving of a little girl with a muff. With all this luxury going on, what hope was there for a better state of things? Instead of lying on a sofa--“My mother is resting,” Elizabeth had said--she should have been in a factory; behind a counter; Mrs. Dalloway and all the other fine ladies!
explanation: A massive block of free indirect discourse that plunges the reader into Miss Kilman's bitter, resentful consciousness. The narrative voice entirely adopts her defensive, self-justifying tone and her associative memories of grievance.
evidence: Defensive internal logic ('First, it was cheap; second, she was over forty'), Rhetorical questions demanding sympathy ('for surely a girl has a right to some kind of happiness?'), Exclamatory outrage ('when the only happy days of her life had been spent in Germany!'), Bitter concluding judgment ('she should have been in a factory; behind a counter; Mrs. Dalloway and all the other fine ladies!')
notes: This is one of the most sustained examples of indirect interior monologue in the novel, perfectly capturing the obsessive, circular nature of Miss Kilman's resentment.

passage: Turning her large gooseberry-coloured eyes upon Clarissa, observing her small pink face, her delicate body, her air of freshness and fashion, Miss Kilman felt, Fool! Simpleton! You who have known neither sorrow nor pleasure; who have trifled your life away! And there rose in her an overmastering desire to overcome her; to unmask her. If she could have felled her it would have eased her. But it was not the body; it was the soul and its mockery that she wished to subdue; make feel her mastery. If only she could make her weep; could ruin her; humiliate her; bring her to her knees crying, You are right! But this was God’s will, not Miss Kilman’s. It was to be a religious victory. So she glared; so she glowered.
explanation: The passage shifts from omniscient description of Miss Kilman's physical gaze into direct interior monologue ('Fool! Simpleton! You who have known...'), then back into an omniscient description of her psychological state ('there rose in her an overmastering desire'), ending with another burst of direct thought ('You are right!').
evidence: Direct, unmediated thought in second person ('Fool! Simpleton! You who have known neither sorrow nor pleasure'), Narratorial psycho-narration ('there rose in her an overmastering desire to overcome her'), Direct imagined dialogue ('bring her to her knees crying, You are right!')

passage: Clarissa was really shocked. This a Christian--this woman! This woman had taken her daughter from her! She in touch with invisible presences! Heavy, ugly, commonplace, without kindness or grace, she know the meaning of life!
explanation: Clarissa's immediate, visceral internal reaction to Miss Kilman's glare. The syntax is highly fragmented and exclamatory, reflecting her shock and revulsion.
evidence: Exclamatory fragments ('This a Christian--this woman!'), Sarcastic internal repetition of Kilman's supposed virtues ('She in touch with invisible presences!'), Harsh subjective adjectives ('Heavy, ugly, commonplace')

passage: Miss Kilman was not going to make herself agreeable. She had always earned her living. Her knowledge of modern history was thorough in the extreme. She did out of her meagre income set aside so much for causes she believed in; whereas this woman did nothing, believed nothing; brought up her daughter--but here was Elizabeth, rather out of breath, the beautiful girl.
explanation: Miss Kilman's internal self-justification continues, contrasting her own perceived virtues with Clarissa's emptiness, before being interrupted by the sight of Elizabeth.
evidence: Internal listing of her own merits ('She had always earned her living. Her knowledge of modern history was thorough'), Harsh internal judgment of Clarissa ('whereas this woman did nothing, believed nothing'), Thought interrupted by perception ('--but here was Elizabeth')

passage: If only she could make her weep; could ruin her; humiliate her; bring her to her knees crying, You are right! But this was God’s will, not Miss Kilman’s. It was to be a religious victory.
explanation: The passage renders Miss Kilman's unspoken desires and rationalizations using third-person pronouns but adopting her exact diction and emotional intensity.
evidence: If only she could make her weep, But this was God’s will, not Miss Kilman’s.

passage: This a Christian--this woman! This woman had taken her daughter from her! She in touch with invisible presences! Heavy, ugly, commonplace, without kindness or grace, she know the meaning of life!
explanation: The narrative drops entirely into Clarissa's unmediated, exclamatory thoughts reacting to Miss Kilman, though it maintains third-person references to Kilman.
evidence: This a Christian--this woman!, she know the meaning of life!
notes: Functions almost as direct interior monologue due to the complete absence of narratorial framing and the raw, exclamatory syntax.

passage: Miss Kilman was not going to make herself agreeable. She had always earned her living. Her knowledge of modern history was thorough in the extreme. She did out of her meagre income set aside so much for causes she believed in; whereas this woman did nothing, believed nothing; brought up her daughter--but here was Elizabeth, rather out of breath, the beautiful girl.
